blocks.body: Common Phrasal Verbs by Topic collects useful expressions for routine, study, work, communication, and typical everyday situations.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"One of the best ways to learn phrasal verbs is to group them by topic instead of alphabetically. This makes it easier to see where you really need them.","rules":[{"label":"Daily routine","color":"emerald","text":"Start with actions you can easily imagine every day: get up, wake up, hurry up, go out.","example":"I get up at 7 and head out at 8."},{"label":"Work and study","color":"blue","text":"Then learn phrasal verbs for tasks and processes: hand in, find out, catch up, carry on.","example":"Please hand in your project by Friday."},{"label":"Problems and communication","color":"amber","text":"In conversation, expressions such as sort out, calm down, speak up, work out appear very often.","example":"We need to sort this out today."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Common phrasal verbs by topic
blocks.body:
Home and daily routine: get up, wake up, put on, take off, clean up.

Travel and movement: get in, get out, set off, pick up, check in.

Social life: drop by, hang out, show up, break up, get along with.

blocks.heading: 2. Study, work, and technology
blocks.body:
Study and work: hand in, find out, catch up on, carry on, look over.

Technology and communication: log in, sign up, turn on, shut down, back up.

Problems and solutions: sort out, work out, deal with, calm down, cut down on.

blocks.heading: 3. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: learning ten new phrasal verbs with no topic.
    Better: collect them in small situation-based groups.
  • Mistake: knowing the translation but not knowing the typical object.
    Better: save a short example such as hand in homework or log in to an account.
  • Mistake: mixing up close expressions such as find out and work out. The first means discover, the second means solve or understand.
  • Mistake: never reusing the phrasal verbs in your own sentences. Without active use, they disappear quickly.

blocks.heading: 4. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Group phrasal verbs by topic, not only by the first verb.
  • Learn each expression together with a typical situation.
  • After learning a topic, write 2-3 of your own sentences to move the expressions into active use.
